title: Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution
content:
\frac{x^2 e^{-x^2/\left(2a^2\right)}}{a^3}</math In <a href="physics" physics</a (in particular in <a href="statistical%20mechanics" statistical mechanics</a), the Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution is a particular <a href="probability%20distribution" probability distribution</a named after <a href="James%20Clerk%20Maxwell" James Clerk Maxwell</a and <a href="Ludwig%20Boltzmann" Ludwig Boltzmann</a.

It was first defined and used for describing particle <a href="speed" speed</a s in <a href="ideal%20gas" idealized gases</a, where the particles move freely inside a stationary container without interacting with one another, except for very brief <a href="collision" collision</a s in which they exchange energy and momentum with each other or with their thermal environment.

The term "particle" in this context refers to gaseous particles only (<a href="atoms" atoms</a or <a href="molecules" molecules</a), and the system of particles is assumed to have reached <a href="thermodynamic%20equilibrium" thermodynamic equilibrium</a.

The energies of such particles follow what is known as <a href="Maxwell%E2%80%93Boltzmann%20statistics" Maxwell–Boltzmann statistics</a, and the statistical distribution of speeds is derived by equating particle energies with <a href="kinetic%20energy" kinetic energy</a.
